data.title: Turkey to host Cop31 climate summit as Australia opts for compromise
data.description:
Turkey will host next year’s UN climate summit while Australia will lead the conference’s negotiations among governments under a compromise deal taking shape in talks in Brazil, Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said on Thursday.
The annual Cop conferences are the world’s main forum for driving climate action. The compromise would resolve a stand-off between Australia and Turkey over who would stage Cop31. Both bid in 2022 to host it and refused to stand down.
The two sides were now close to a deal that would see Turkey hosting Cop31 as summit president, with a pre-Cop event staged in the Pacific and Australia as president of negotiations, Albanese said.
“What we’ve come up with is a big win for both Australia and Turkey,” he told Australian Broadcasting Corporation Radio.
The two nations now have just a year to prepare for an event that attracts tens of thousands of people and requires months of diplomatic legwork to reach consensus around climate goals.
“There’s a little way to go in these discussions,” Australia’s Climate Change and Energy Minister Chris Bowen told reporters at Cop30 in Belem, Brazil, adding that the compromise would achieve Australia’s aims.
“It would be great if Australia could have it all. But we can’t have it all,” he said. “It was important to strike an agreement.”


The Turkish government did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Australia has pitched its bid as a “Pacific Cop”, done in partnership with low-lying island nations and emphasising their exposure to climate change and rising sea levels.
It says it has already spent A$7 million (US$4.5 million) on preparing to host, reflecting confidence that backing from a large number of countries would enable it to fend off Turkey’s bid.
Turkey has said that as an emerging economy it would promote solidarity between rich and poor countries at its summit, which would have a more global rather than regional focus.
Earlier this week, Albanese rejected prospects of co-hosting the event, citing United Nations rules. Turkey had urged such a joint model and said the sides had discussed potential frameworks in September.
A source familiar with the discussions said there was some confusion about the arrangement on running the negotiations and whether the sharing of responsibilities was permissible under UN rules that envisage one country leading.
The person declined to be named as they are not authorised to speak to the media. News of the compromise deal was first reported by Bloomberg.

data.title: Hong Kong’s elite schools vet book donations for national security risks
data.description:
An elite Hong Kong government school has warned that some books donated by alumni may contain material potentially breaching national security legislation, pledging to adopt a new policy rejecting publications with political propaganda or illegal content.
King’s College, in Mid-Levels, revealed the decision in its latest annual report on safeguarding national security and promoting national security education.
In its report, the 99-year-old institution said many alumni and supporters had donated books to show their backing, but it found that some titles potentially risked breaching the national security law and had decided to introduce a mechanism to handle them.
“Some of these older books were published many years ago and the origins of the books were unknown. They may not be suitable for current educational development and student growth and may even contain content that could potentially violate the Hong Kong national security law,” it said.
Hong Kong’s national security law, imposed by Beijing in 2020, bans acts of collusion with foreign countries to endanger national security, secession, subversion and terrorist activities.
Since the 2022-23 academic year, schools have been required to fully implement administrative and educational measures on safeguarding national security and related education.

They must also submit work plans and annual reports to the Education Bureau after securing approval from their governing bodies.
“[We] suggest setting up a donation policy on ‘books or multimedia materials accepted by the school’ to list out the categories that can be accepted (such as the year of publication, themes, language, etc),” the report by King’s College said.
“It must also clearly spell out that books containing political propaganda or illegal content will not be accepted.”
It added that the policy aimed to avoid risks arising from well-intentioned donations.
Queen Elizabeth School in Mong Kok, another elite government school, said in its report that all subject panel heads in the last academic year had helped the library officer review the existing collections to remove “inappropriate” books from the shelves and were consulted before subscribing to electronic books.
It said all donated books would be screened by subject panel heads to ensure the content was appropriate.
“The books would then have to be approved by the school management committee before being accepted,” it said.
The 71-year-old school said it would purchase books introducing the country’s latest developments.

St Paul’s Co-educational College, another top secondary school in Hong Kong, said in its latest report on national security education that it would ensure all materials bought complied with the education authorities’ guidelines.
“Reference materials related to the concept of national security, recent development of the motherland and Hong Kong were bought to enrich the school library resources,” it said.
Under current guidance issued by the Education Bureau and updated in 2023, schools must ensure that books and other resources – including printed and electronic library collections, electronic and online learning materials, publications and leaflets – do not contain content that endangers national security.
“If these curriculum resources are found to involve serious crimes or moral impropriety, potentially breach the law or contain elements that endanger national security or are contrary to the interests of national security, they should be removed or withdrawn from use immediately so as to avoid causing negative impacts on students,” the bureau said.

data.title: Stop deep-frying turkeys for Thanksgiving, it’s dry and dangerous
data.description:
With American Thanksgiving around the corner, I need to call out one of the dumbest cooking trends the internet has ever promoted: deep-frying turkeys. It is time to stop!
This big bad idea began back in the late 1970s. Southern chefs in New Orleans, who normally fried chickens, decided to apply their technique to a different bird five times the size.
As time went by, celebrity cooks like Martha Stewart and Emeril Lagasse took the scheme to their television shows, adding to the lore.
But it was YouTube – making the world ever more stupid – that really elevated this sizzling shenanigan to epic proportions. The concept of cooking a large turkey in a fraction of the oven time, not to mention the redneck spectacle of a pot of hot oil, a wrecking ball of a fowl, and the ever imminent danger of this combustible combination spilling and splattering into a backyard fireball, was just too irresistible not to click.

Some culinary proponents argue that deep-frying makes the bird crispier and more tender. I am not so sure. Turkey has so little fat that the skin is likely to be brittle, not crunchy. The one time I tasted a fried turkey, the breast meat was as dry as a manila envelope.
Let us face it, its popularity has nothing to do with gastronomy. The deep-fried turkey trend is a recipe for bros and dudes to disguise testosterone jocularity as an act of domestic activity. The risk of burning down the house is just part of the fun.
If you are now browsing for some of these turkey-frying tutorials and clips online, I would also recommend a funny cautionary video with William Shatner, made by the insurance company State Farm, that highlights the hazards of dunking a huge turkey in a cauldron of scalding oil.
Numerous fire services across America have also issued warnings against doing this. Fortunately, few people are dumb enough to try it in Hong Kong.

But if you must give it a try, take caution. First, do it outdoors. I cannot believe how many fools have set their kitchen on fire with hot grease.
Next, do not put so much oil in the pot that it spills out or splashes when you drop the turkey in.
Also important is to turn off the burner when the bird is gently lowered in, to reduce the risk of a flame if oil touches fuel.
It is also crucial to thaw the bird completely and dry the exterior. Water and oil do not mix, unless you are ready to give thanks for a bonfire. According to the US National Fire Protection Association, five people die each year from deep-fryer accidents.
Another potential disaster is that the turkey is not cooked all the way through, resulting in raw meat, salmonella and diarrhoea for everyone.
Lastly, do not follow the example of US Secretary of Health and Human Services Robert F. Kennedy Jr and deep-fry a turkey while barefoot. Do I even need to explain?

If you insist on a different cooking method for your boring Thanksgiving turkey, try this Asian alternative from Taiwan, which is based on Hainanese chicken rice.
Poach the turkey, broken down for ease, in spring onion, ginger, rice wine and a tasty broth, making it more moist and flavourful than oven-roasting. Cook the rice in the same broth, and accompany the shredded meat with classic Hainan chicken dipping sauces or go wild with a sweet-and-sour cranberry jus. Voilà!
You are welcome.

data.title: Young Chinese fans fear losing access to Japanese pop culture amid diplomatic row
data.description:
The Japanese comedy Cells at Work! was expected to be released in Chinese cinemas on Saturday.
The live-action movie is adapted from a popular manga series and follows the slapstick adventures of cells inside the body of a Japanese teenager.
However, the movie’s launch date in China has been pushed back. Also postponed is the release of Crayon Shinchan the Movie: Super Hot! The Spicy Kasukabe Dancers, an animated film that was scheduled to hit Chinese screens in early December.
The delays come in the wake of a diplomatic row between the two countries – and more could follow, with calls online for boycotts of Japanese cultural exports and the spectre of further event cancellations.


Many young Chinese have grown up with broad access to Japanese animation, comics and games as well as films and television dramas.
Among the most popular is the work of Studio Ghibli’s Hayao Miyazaki, director of films such as Spirited Away and Howl’s Moving Castle.
Concerts featuring music by Studio Ghibli score composer Joe Hisaishi have also long drawn enthusiastic audiences. And earlier this year, 42 performances of the original Japanese-language stage adaptation of Spirited Away were sold out in Shanghai, according to media reports.
The mood has soured, though, with Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s remarks this month suggesting that Tokyo could deploy its military forces in the event of a conflict in the Taiwan Strait.
Beijing lodged strong protests over her comments, describing them as “a blatant interference in China’s internal affairs”.
The row has already affected dialogue between the two countries.
The annual Beijing-Tokyo Forum, which regularly draws high-profile officials from both sides and had been scheduled for this weekend in Beijing, has been postponed.
Chinese foreign ministry spokeswoman Mao Ning said on Monday the forum had been postponed after consultation between the co-hosts. Mao added that Takaichi’s remarks had “poisoned public opinion”.
In addition, China has urged travellers and students to reconsider plans to go to Japan.
There is a very strong precedent in China for taking such action against a neighbour.
In 2016, South Korean films, TV programmes and music were hit by an unofficial boycott after the country approved the deployment of the US Terminal High-Altitude Area Defence missile system in the country, which Beijing saw as a security threat.

For Wang Shu, a 21-year-old university student in Tianjin who has followed Japanese animation since childhood, the current climate is “very unfriendly” to exchanges in the arts.
Wang, who has attended animation conventions and occasionally dresses up for cosplay, said she was concerned that public sentiment could turn against fans like her.
“General public opinion might become unfriendly and turn to criticise people who follow [the animation, comics and games] culture as a hobby,” she said.
Amid the tensions, some Chinese have expressed support online for measures targeting Japanese cultural exports.
“Starting with ourselves, the whole nation should resist Japan,” one up-voted commenter said.
Several others called for restraint, noting that they preferred a nuanced and personal approach to cultural consumption.
“When I choose games, anime or related merchandise, I … check whether the company has ever made anti-China remarks or said anything inappropriate on sensitive issues,” said Xu, a 22-year-old fan of Hello Kitty products and Nintendo games.
“If relations continue to worsen, upcoming events might be cancelled, which would be inconvenient and disappointing for fans.”
On social media platforms, many have raised concerns about the possible withdrawal of Demon Slayer: Infinity Castle, a hit Japanese animated fantasy released in China over the weekend. The film has topped the Chinese box office, taking more than 400 million yuan (US$56.3 million) in revenue by Wednesday, according to Lighthouse Professional Edition, a Chinese box office website.
“If the rumoured withdrawal happens, I’ll make sure to watch it before it’s taken down,” Xu said.
Some internet users also raised concerns over the possible postponement of a concert featuring Japanese singers in Shanghai this weekend and a musical adaptation of the Sailor Moon manga which will begin touring Hangzhou, Shanghai and Beijing later this month.
Wu Yuening, a 21-year-old university student in Beijing, said the diplomatic tensions would not affect her decision whether to attend an event, adding that she thought “excessive cultural restrictions could only deepen tensions”.
We used to have a concept of a global village, but now it feels like we’re drifting further away
Pan, Chinese fan of Japanese culture
Simon Guo, who follows Japanese animation and likes related themed concerts, pointed to the high degree of collaboration between artists and staff on both sides, adding that creative work should not be conflated with politics.
“A lot of Chinese people work in Japan’s anime industry … and for films released in China, they surely involve many Chinese contributors’ efforts in every aspect,” said Guo, a 30-year-old working in the indie gaming industry in Shanghai.
Another consumer, Pan, a fan of Japanese drama and the anime series One Piece, argued that public sentiment alone was likely to have limited influence, unless there was a shift in official policies.
“If it’s just the public’s negative emotions … Keyboard warriors online will eventually shift their attention elsewhere, or they will find another outlet for their negative emotions,” she said.
However, concerns remained regarding the broader cultural and social environment, said Pan, who is a frequent visitor to Japan.
“I am worried that the anti-intellectual emotions could spread and obstruct normal cultural exchange. We used to have a concept of a global village, but now it feels like we’re drifting further away,” she said.
“I hope things become more open … if cultural life becomes more isolated, this could also drive talent away.”

data.title: In Indonesia, US and Canada, ‘online hoaxes’ fuel measles deaths
data.description:
Indonesia has launched emergency vaccination drives after measles outbreaks killed more than 20 children in two provinces, stoking fears of a wider epidemic amid a global resurgence of the virus.
The scale and speed of the outbreak have alarmed health experts, who warn the crisis is not simply the result of faltering immunisation coverage, but also a symptom of growing vaccine hesitancy – fuelled by online misinformation, religious concerns and a legacy of public distrust.
This erosion of confidence now threatens to overwhelm efforts to contain further spread.

Between January and August, Indonesia’s Health Ministry confirmed more than 3,400 measles cases across 14 provinces, nearly matching the 3,500 cases recorded for the whole of last year. Thousands more suspected cases are under investigation, prompting authorities to designate the outbreak “an extraordinary event”.
In East Java’s Sumenep, the disease has already claimed at least 20 lives. Another hotspot, Riau province, had reported 900 suspected cases and 142 confirmed as of the end of October, including the death of a junior school pupil in the provincial capital, Pekanbaru.
“The transmission rate is very high,” warned Hazil Fendriyanto, head of the Pekanbaru Health Agency, on November 10. “We urge the public to be aware of disease transmission.”
‘Online hoaxes’
Measles, a highly contagious viral illness, spreads easily through respiratory droplets when infected people cough or sneeze.
Even a single case in a community with low immunisation can cause a cascade of infections, according to Dr Dicky Budiman, a public health expert and epidemiologist at Yarsi University in Jakarta.
Symptoms, including a high fever, sore throat, fatigue and runny nose, often resemble a common cold, before giving way to a distinctive red rash that can engulf the entire body.
Budiman said vaccine hesitancy was not unique to Indonesia, citing the current outbreaks in the United States and Canada that also “have large populations with low vaccination rates in some areas”.

“Populations in these countries have been influenced by online hoaxes and conspiracy theories,” he told This Week in Asia. “There are pockets of vulnerability in some communities and, if people travel outside of these communities, they can make the virus spread.”
Canada has reported more than 5,000 measles cases this year, up from just 147 in 2024, leading to two provinces – Ontario and Alberta – losing their “measles-free” status for the first time in three decades. The US has also seen over a thousand cases this year, with the Centres for Disease Control linking the surge to flagging immunisation rates.
In Indonesia, vaccine hesitancy is a growing concern among healthcare workers. In North Sumatra’s capital, Medan, paediatrician Dr Steven Guntur said parents’ trust in immunisations had wavered.
“It is true that there is a decline in parental trust in immunisations in some regions and online hoaxes are a significant factor,” he said.
“Some parents read or hear that vaccines are unsafe, can cause serious side effects or have hidden agendas. This makes them afraid and they delay or forgo vaccinating their children.”

When health workers failed to provide clear, accessible information, families were left susceptible to scaremongering, Guntur said, adding that some parents believed illnesses like measles were rare they saw little reason to vaccinate their children.
Religious and cultural factors further complicate the situation. With 87 per cent of Indonesians identifying as Muslim, doubts over the halal status of vaccines have historically hampered immunisation campaigns.
Past health scandals have deepened the distrust, few more devastating than a scandal in 2022 involving tainted cough and fever syrups contaminated with toxic industrial solvents that claimed 157 lives, mostly children.
In Bekasi, outside Jakarta, nurse Tia Rizki said she wanted her three-year-old daughter vaccinated “to lower the risk” – but her husband still refused to do so after their son’s death in the poisoning scandal.
My husband doesn’t believe in vaccines
Tia Rizki, Indonesian nurse
“My husband doesn’t believe in vaccines,” she told This Week in Asia. “My husband says that if a child is vaccinated or not, it is all the same.”
Health experts, however, stress that robust immunisation is the only way to halt the current outbreak and protect vulnerable communities.
“We need to deal with the current outbreak with a rapid response and vaccination programme,” Budiman said.
“My recommendation is vaccinations focused on particularly vulnerable or susceptible communities and clear communication of the risks to the population using simple and locally appropriate language about the safety and efficacy of vaccines.”

data.title: Trump vows to end Sudan war, in sudden pivot thanks to Saudi crown prince
data.description:
US President Donald Trump vowed Wednesday to end Sudan’s grinding civil war at the request of Saudi Arabia’s crown prince, condemning “tremendous atrocities” in a conflict he has previously overlooked.
Trump admitted that the devastating war between Sudan’s army and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF) was “not on my charts” before Prince Mohammed bin Salman pushed him to get involved.
But Trump said he would now work to “stabilise” the conflict with regional powers, notably including the United Arab Emirates, which denies accusations of backing the RSF with weapons and mercenaries.
The United Nations has repeatedly called for greater global attention to the war, which has killed tens of thousands of people and displaced nearly 12 million since its outbreak in April 2023.
“His majesty would like me to do something very powerful having to do with Sudan,” Trump said at a business forum with the Saudi royal a day after Prince Mohammed received a lavish reception at the White House.

“It was not on my charts to be involved in, I thought it was just something that was crazy and out of control. But I just see how important that is to you, and to a lot of your friends in the room, Sudan. And we’re going to start working on Sudan.”
Sudan’s Saudi-backed sovereign council, which is headed by army chief Abdel Fattah al-Burhan, said it was ready to cooperate with the United States and Riyadh.
In a statement, the council thanked Washington and Riyadh for “their continued efforts to stop Sudanese bloodshed.”
International attention on the conflict has increased since the RSF recently seized the key Darfur city of El-Fasher after a relentless siege that has sparked warnings of crimes against humanity and genocide.
Trump had barely commented on the Sudan war in the nine months since he returned to office, focusing instead on the wars in Gaza and Ukraine.
But on Wednesday, the 79-year-old Republican said he would use the “influence of the presidency to bring an immediate halt” to the war.
“Tremendous atrocities are taking place in Sudan. It has become the most violent place on Earth,” Trump said on social media a few hours after his initial comments.
“We will work with Saudi Arabia, United Arab Emirates, Egypt, and other Middle Eastern partners to get these atrocities to end, while at the same time stabilising Sudan.”


While the conflict has been off Trump’s radar, Washington has stepped up efforts in recent months to resolve it.
US Secretary of State Marco Rubio called the UAE’s foreign minister on Friday to urge Abu Dhabi to back a Sudan ceasefire.
Trump’s own Africa envoy Massad Boulos on Saturday said that the war in Sudan was the “world’s biggest humanitarian crisis”.
Trump repeatedly claims to have solved eight conflicts since returning to office in January as he seeks a Nobel Peace Prize.
His promise to start working on the Sudan conflict reflects his close ties with the de facto Saudi leader, whom he hosted at the White House for a lavish visit on Tuesday.
Their closeness was also underscored by his comments in the Oval Office on Tuesday, during which Trump defended the prince over the 2018 murder of Washington Post journalist Jamal Khashoggi, saying the prince “knew nothing”.

data.title: China’s clinical trial capacity matches US, attracts more global pharma firms, investors
data.description:
China’s growing pipeline of novel drug candidates is expected to draw increased attention from global pharmaceutical companies and investors scouting for licensing opportunities, as the country’s clinical trial capacity has expanded to match those in the US.
That assessment was made by Shanghai-based Helen Chen, Greater China managing partner at L.E.K. Consulting, one of the most respected analysts in the mainland’s life sciences industry.
“There is increased interest from multinational corporations in conducting investigator-initiated trials for cell and gene therapies in China, given that they can have access to patient data more rapidly than in other markets,” Chen said.
Such trials refer to clinical studies led by academic researchers or physicians – instead of pharmaceutical companies – often to explore new uses for treatments.
As of 2024, China’s clinical trial volumes matched 80 per cent of those in the US and exceeded the levels in Europe by 10 per cent, according to a report jointly published last week by L.E.K. Consulting and PharmaDJ.
The report, which polled 89 pharmaceutical and contract research organisation leaders between August and September, showed China had 2,694 new clinical trials in 2024, up 13 per cent from a year earlier.
Multinational companies accounted for 14.3 per cent of those clinical trials in the country last year, representing a 7 per cent compound annual growth rate since 2019. Most of the trials in China were launched by domestic pharmaceutical firms, some of which have global drug ambitions.
“We don’t see multinational firms slowing down their clinical trials in China due to geopolitical tensions,” Chen said. “As the second largest pharmaceutical market globally, China remains an important market for these multinational companies.”

“The renewed growth of innovative clinical trials suggests that the pipeline of biotech firms is getting more robust,” she added.
These developments reflected China’s push towards greater biomedical manufacturing as part of technological self-reliance.
Biomedicine was identified by Beijing as a key pillar to enhance domestic industrial supply chains, forming part of the country’s next five-year development blueprint, according to a JPMorgan report dated October 29.
In the coming years, the investment bank expected Chinese policymakers to provide more targeted support for research and development and commercialisation in the biomedical manufacturing sector.
Meanwhile, Chen pointed out that the larger number of clinical studies in China translated to “more assets to be discovered by pharma search teams for licensing”, as well as more opportunities for investors to set up new companies on the mainland.
Many major deals between multinational firms and domestic biotech companies “are built on China-only data”, said Dai Jialing Dai, PharmaDJ’s president and publisher, in a statement. He asserted that China would remain an important market for clinical development for both global pharmaceutical firms and domestic biotech companies.
Chinese pharmaceutical companies’ sale of early-stage assets to global partners soared to nearly US$70 billion as of September 2025, an eightfold increase from the level of transactions in 2021, according to the L.E.K. Consulting and PharmaDJ report.
Over the past five years, China’s out-licensing deals have expanded into a wider range of therapeutic modalities, according to the report. These mostly focus on cancer treatments such as “guided missile-like” chemotherapy antibody-drug conjugates, bispecific antibodies that target two disease pathways and cell therapies in which living cells are engineered to fight or repair disease.
Total research spending by China’s top 50 biopharmaceutical companies by revenue reached US$8.5 billion last year, which marked a 12 per cent compound annual growth rate since 2021, the report said.
“This could mean a substantive upgrade on the importance of biotech or pharma companies,” the report said.

data.title: Israeli air strikes kill 25 in Gaza, rattling ceasefire
data.description:
At least 25 Palestinians were killed in four Israeli air strikes on Wednesday in a part of Gaza under Hamas control since a shaky ceasefire took effect in October, health authorities said.
The Israeli military said its forces struck Hamas targets across Gaza after members of the Palestinian militant group fired on its troops in violation of the nearly six-week-old ceasefire. No Israeli forces were injured.
Hamas condemned the Israeli strikes as a dangerous escalation, and urged the United States to “honour its stated commitments and exert immediate pressure on Israel to enforce the ceasefire and halt its attacks”.
But a US official, who spoke anonymously, said Hamas was aiming to break the ceasefire and not fulfil its commitment to demilitarise.
“These desperate tactics will fail,” the official said.

Medical workers said 10 people were killed in the Gaza City suburb of Zeitoun, two in the Shejaia suburb to the east, and the rest in two separate attacks in Khan Younis in the southern Gaza Strip.
Repeated shooting incidents have pointed to the fragility of the ceasefire. Israel and Hamas have traded blame for what both call violations of the US-brokered truce, the first stage of President Donald Trump’s 20-point plan for a post-war Gaza.
All four attacks were far beyond an agreed-upon imaginary “yellow line” separating the areas under Israeli and Palestinian control, according to medical staff, witnesses and Palestinian media.
The Zeitoun attack was on a building belonging to Muslim religious authorities and the Khan Younis attack was on a UN-run club, both of which house displaced families.
The October 10 ceasefire in the two-year Gaza war has eased the conflict, enabling hundreds of thousands of Palestinians to return to Gaza’s ruins. Israel has pulled troops back from city positions, and aid flows have increased.
But violence has not completely halted. Palestinian health authorities say Israeli forces have killed 305 people in strikes on Gaza since the truce, nearly half of them in one day last week when Israel retaliated for an attack on its troops.
Israel says three of its soldiers have been killed since the ceasefire began and it has targeted scores of fighters.

data.title: China’s bond market gains as central bank resumes debt purchases amid growth concerns
data.description:
China’s bond market is set to gain further momentum, buoyed by the central bank’s resumption of government debt purchases, as softer economic data and downward pressure on growth necessitate further policy easing, according to analysts.
Huayuan Securities forecast that the upward trend was likely to drive the yield on China’s benchmark 10-year sovereign bond down to 1.65 per cent within this year. Southwest Securities predicted a drop to 1.7 per cent. The yield currently stands at 1.808 per cent.
Economic data for October indicated a worsening outlook for growth, bolstering the case for monetary easing. Industrial production grew less than expected, fixed-asset investment shrank, retail sales slowed for a fifth consecutive month, and declines in housing prices showed no signs of abating.
Expectations for policy loosening in China have also strengthened amid hopes for more rate cuts in the US, which would align the monetary policies of the world’s two largest economies to mitigate foreign exchange volatility.
“Given the weak economic data and falling property prices, there is an increasing urgency for the policy rate to fall,” said Liao Zhiming, an analyst at Huayuan Securities. The firm anticipated a 20-basis-point cut in the policy rate in the next six months, which would support the bond market in the fourth quarter.
“We are positive on bonds,” Liao said.

China’s bonds had underperformed relative to stocks until recently, as enthusiasm for artificial intelligence and improved Sino-US relations drove investors towards riskier assets.
The yield on 10-year debt rose to as high as 1.905 per cent in September, increasing by 28 basis points over four months. However, the trend reversed last month when the People’s Bank of China resumed buying government bonds after a nine-month hiatus, injecting more liquidity into the market.
ING suggested that Beijing might avoid significant stimulus this year to preserve policy tools, as the annual growth target of about 5 per cent appeared attainable this year. Still, the need for strengthened monetary and fiscal support for the economy was intensifying following the November data release, the Dutch bank said.
Nomura Holdings expected a 10-basis-point cut in borrowing costs by year-end to stimulate short-term growth.
Investor sentiment is shifting in favour of bonds, as the stock rally shows signs of fatigue amid concerns about inflated technology stock valuations and excessive investment in AI. A benchmark tracking the 50 largest stocks on the tech-heavy Star Market in Shanghai has fallen over 10 per cent in the past month.
Xiangcai Securities recommended that investors adopt a dip-buying strategy for bond investments, with the yield on the 10-year bond expected to fluctuate between 1.75 per cent and 1.85 per cent in the near term.
China’s bond market saw its largest bull run in a decade last year, as investors flocked to safe-haven assets in response to a rapidly deteriorating growth outlook. Strong demand pushed the yield on government debt maturing in 10 years down by 88.5 basis points in 2024, prompting a warning from the central bank that gains had been too rapid.
“The economic data is weakening and market expectations for loosening are building,” said Yang Jiefeng, an analyst at Southwest Securities. “That’ll boost sentiment for long positions in bonds as the year comes to a close.”

data.title: Male vs female hairy crabs: which are tastiest? Hong Kong chefs peel back the differences
data.description:
Autumn signals the start of the hairy crab season in Hong Kong and parts of mainland China.
A question that divides hairy crab lovers is: are males or females the most delicious?
When it comes to the flavour of the crabs, seasonality matters a lot. Male crabs hit their peak in the 10th lunar month, when they develop plump, smooth gonads. The 11th lunar month is the female crabs’ time to shine as their roe ripens and becomes firmer.
Four Hong Kong chefs compare male and female hairy crabs further to help us understand what makes each desirable.

Junno Li, executive Chinese chef at The Chinese Library in Hong Kong’s Tai Kwun centre of heritage and culture, says that female crabs are prized for their rich-tasting roe.
“The female crab roe is rich, creamy and has a deep golden colour. The flavour is intense and buttery, making it perfect if you like something more robust,” he says.
“Male crabs, on the other hand, do not have roe; instead, they have white, jellylike reproductive glands that are incredibly silky and delicate on the palate, and the taste is much lighter and more subtle.”
Menex Cheung, executive chef of China Tang in the Landmark in Central, echoes that a key difference between male and female crabs is the richness of the females’ roe.
“Male crabs have creamy [reproductive glands] … while female crabs have yellow crab roe and are known for their rich crab roe aroma,” he says.

Another key difference is the meat texture.
“Male crabs are generally larger in size and therefore have relatively more plump meat in their crab claws and feet,” says Cheung Kin-ming, executive chef of two-Michelin-star Ying Jee Club.
He adds that the meat from both males and females is fresh and sweet. “But the point of tasting crab is always its roe and accessory glands.”
Why do hairy crabs tend to get more attention than other types of seasonal crustaceans? Silas Li, executive chef of Hong Kong Cuisine 1983 in Happy Valley, feels that there is a certain prestige attached to the act of eating hairy crabs.
“It’s the luxury of eating this rare delicacy. The flavour is so intense and the texture is so smooth. There is also a lot of labour involved when you’re eating [them] at a restaurant. It takes the chefs hours to deshell the crabs and turn them into a dish,” he says.

Menex Cheung feels that the female crab roe holds more cultural value. “The crab roe represents abundance and preciousness, as the crab roe is treated as the most delicious and precious part of the crab. Its richness and delicate flavour are the best showcase of Chinese gastronomy,” he says.
Cheung Kin-ming feels it is about seasonality.
“First, this embodies the philosophy of eating within the seasons, and savouring hairy crabs is a way to appreciate the unique richness of autumn,” he says.
“Second, Chinese culinary culture places great emphasis on the texture of ingredients. The powdery and glutinous texture of crab roe and the rich and sticky texture of crab accessory glands offer a unique taste experience that is difficult to match with other ingredients.”

For those new to hairy crab, most chefs recommend starting with the more punchy-flavoured females.
“You can really taste the richness with female crabs,” Silas Li says. “The sticky pastiness of the male may take some getting used to, but the strong flavours of the female are something that stand out.”
However, Menex Cheung suggests first-timers eat male crabs as he feels the flavour of the females could be too strong for the uninitiated.
“I usually recommend male crabs for their plump and rich meat, [which makes it easier for] newcomers to appreciate the crabs’ freshness and creaminess. Female crabs might be too greasy for a first-timer due to their oiliness and richness of crab roe,” he says.

The chefs have different ideas on how to contrast the flavours of male and female hairy crabs in a multi-course set.
“In a multi-course crab menu, I’d design it so the two crabs play different roles,” Junno Li says.
“The male crab’s clean, silky reproductive glands and the female crab’s rich, buttery roe each get their moment, prepared with different techniques to highlight what makes them special.
“You’d have texture contrasts, the thickness of roe against the silkiness of male glands, as well as flavour contrasts, with the richness of female against the fresh sweetness of male. By layering all these contrasts, diners get the full spectrum of what each crab offers.”

Menex Cheung places importance on the order in which the crabs are eaten.
“I suggest having male crabs to begin the meal. The freshness and natural sweetness of crabmeat would be a good beginning of the meal, and enjoying the rich and delicate female crab roe afterwards fully showcases hairy crabs’ distinctive tastes and multifaceted allure,” he says.
Silas Li feels that things might not have to be so polarising.
“There are certain dishes like dumplings and toppings where the female roe is better, and the richness of the male is better used in mains, but I like to mix them together. In the same bite, you can enjoy the best of both male and female crab – the strong flavour of the females and then the rich texture of the male,” he says.

Cheung Kin-ming has a more methodical approach.
“For the appetiser, crab roe from female crabs can be used to make crab roe soup dumplings and other dim sum. Then the crabs can be presented whole and steamed to allow guests to savour the cleanest flavour from both male and female crabs,” he says.
“Then, the roe from male crabs is used to make rice or noodles, utilising its rich and oily qualities to create a memorable experience for the crab feast.
“Finally, the crab shells can be simmered into a delicious soup, blending the flavours of both male and female crabs, providing a perfect ending.”
Whichever you prefer, Junno Li says that hairy crabs are more than just something delicious to eat.
“They represent the essence of the season, the pinnacle of what an ingredient can offer, and a kind of respect for nature’s rhythms.”
